event: The earliest known human settlers, the Archaic (pre-Arawak) peoples, arrive on the island and live as hunter-gatherers and fishers.
Saladoid Arawak peoples migrate from South America via the Lesser Antilles, introducing pottery, agriculture, and village life.
The Ostionoid culture develops, marking the transition toward the later Taino civilization on the island.
The Taino civilization flourishes, organizing the island (called Boriken) into chiefdoms led by caciques with ceremonial ball courts and yuca-based agriculture.
Christopher Columbus lands on the island during his second voyage and claims it for Spain, naming it San Juan Bautista.
Juan Ponce de Leon establishes the first Spanish settlement at Caparra and becomes the island's first governor.
Taino chiefs led by Agueybana II launch a major rebellion against Spanish rule, which is brutally suppressed.
The capital is relocated to a new fortified settlement named Puerto Rico, later San Juan, while the island takes the name Puerto Rico.
Construction begins on the fortress of La Fortaleza and later El Morro to defend San Juan against rival European powers.
English privateer Sir Francis Drake attacks San Juan but is repelled by the city's fortifications.
English forces under George Clifford briefly capture San Juan before disease forces them to withdraw.
Dutch forces under Boudewijn Hendricksz besiege and burn much of San Juan but fail to take El Morro.
Alejandro O'Reilly conducts a royal survey and reforms, spurring economic and military reorganization of the colony.
A large British expedition under Ralph Abercromby attempts to seize San Juan but is decisively repulsed.
Puerto Rico is granted the right to send a representative to the Spanish Cortes, recognizing it as a province of Spain.
The Royal Decree of Graces opens the island to foreign Catholic immigrants and trade, expanding the sugar and coffee plantation economy.
The Grito de Lares uprising proclaims a short-lived independent republic before being quickly suppressed by Spanish authorities.
Spain abolishes slavery in Puerto Rico, freeing roughly 30,000 enslaved people of African descent.
Spain grants Puerto Rico an Autonomic Charter providing substantial self-government within the Spanish Empire.
The United States invades during the Spanish-American War, and Spain cedes Puerto Rico to the U.S. under the Treaty of Paris.
The Foraker Act establishes a civilian government with a U.S.-appointed governor and limited local legislature.
The Jones-Shafroth Act grants U.S. citizenship to Puerto Ricans and creates an elected Senate.
Hurricane San Felipe Segundo devastates the island, killing hundreds and ruining the coffee and sugar harvests.
The Ponce Massacre sees police kill nineteen people at a Nationalist Party march for independence.
Congress passes the Elective Governor Act, allowing Puerto Ricans to elect their own governor for the first time.
Luis Munoz Marin is elected the first popularly chosen governor and launches the Operation Bootstrap industrialization program.
Nationalist uprisings break out across the island, and two Nationalists attempt to assassinate President Truman in Washington.
Puerto Rico adopts its own constitution and becomes the Commonwealth (Estado Libre Asociado) of the United States.
Puerto Rican Nationalists open fire in the U.S. House of Representatives, wounding five congressmen.
A plebiscite is held on political status, with voters favoring the continuation of commonwealth status.
Baseball legend Roberto Clemente dies in a plane crash while delivering aid to earthquake victims in Nicaragua.
A status plebiscite again narrowly favors commonwealth over statehood and independence.
Hurricane Georges strikes the island, causing widespread destruction, while a status referendum produces no clear majority.
The accidental killing of a civilian guard sparks mass protests demanding the U.S. Navy leave the island of Vieques.
The U.S. Navy ends its bombing exercises and withdraws from Vieques after decades of training there.
A status referendum shows a majority rejecting commonwealth status, with statehood favored among stated alternatives.
Facing a debt crisis, Congress enacts the PROMESA law creating a federal oversight board to manage Puerto Rico's finances.
Hurricane Maria devastates Puerto Rico, causing prolonged blackouts and thousands of deaths in one of the deadliest U.S. disasters.
Massive protests over leaked chat messages and corruption force Governor Ricardo Rossello to resign.
A series of strong earthquakes strikes the southwestern region, damaging infrastructure and displacing residents.
Hurricane Fiona triggers an island-wide power blackout and major flooding, exposing the fragility of the rebuilt electric grid.
